title: Fix Dining Philosophers visualization — visual lie shows wrong state for 4 of 5 philosophers
description:
VISUAL LIE (V1=1/3): ConcurrencyModule.tsx:392-393 uses `event.philosopherId === id ? event.state : 'thinking'`, which defaults all non-active philosophers to 'thinking' regardless of actual state. At any step, only the philosopher mentioned in the current event shows their true state. This means a student watching the deadlock scenario sees 4 philosophers as 'thinking' when they are actually 'waiting' with forks held.

FIX: Build a cumulative state map from events[0..stepIndex]. For each philosopher, track their last known state across all events up to the current step. Use this map instead of the single-event lookup.

This is the P0 visual lie — a student LEARNING FROM THIS will misunderstand what deadlock looks like because most philosophers appear to be thinking when they're actually stuck waiting.
acceptanceCriteria: All 5 philosopher circles show their CORRECT state at every step (not just the active one)
When deadlock occurs, all 5 circles show 'waiting' state simultaneously (not 4 'thinking' + 1 'waiting')
Fork ownership indicators match the cumulative state map
Both naive and ordered tables use the same cumulative state tracking

title: Fix Sleeping Barber tick double-increment corrupting step generation timing
description:
STEP GENERATION BUG (I5=1/3): sleeping-barber.ts:191 has an unconditional `tick++` at the end of the while loop body, in addition to `tick++` inside each event push. This causes: (1) tick gaps in the event sequence, (2) the `tick % ARRIVAL_INTERVAL === 0` check at line 93-94 misses expected arrival ticks, (3) customer arrivals are irregularly spaced instead of every 2 ticks.

FIX: Remove the unconditional `tick++` at line 191. Each event already increments tick when pushed. The while loop's `tick < maxTicks` termination condition still works because events increment tick.

VERIFY after fix: customer arrivals occur at exactly every ARRIVAL_INTERVAL ticks, tick numbers in events are sequential without gaps.
acceptanceCriteria: Remove unconditional tick++ at sleeping-barber.ts:191
Event tick numbers are sequential without gaps
Customers arrive at exactly every ARRIVAL_INTERVAL ticks
Simulation still terminates (all customers served or turned away)

title: Add pseudocode display to Race Condition implementation
description:
I6=0/3: No pseudocode exists. The race condition is the most fundamental concurrency concept and students need to see the canonical pseudocode to understand the read-modify-write pattern.

ADD pseudocode:
```
// UNSAFE (no synchronization)
for each thread:
  local = read(counter)     // stale read possible
  counter = local + 1       // last writer wins

// SAFE (mutex-protected)
for each thread:
  lock(mutex)
  local = read(counter)     // protected read
  counter = local + 1       // no interleaving possible
  unlock(mutex)
```

Display in the properties panel or a collapsible code panel adjacent to the visualization.
acceptanceCriteria: Pseudocode for both unsafe and safe versions shown
Pseudocode is language-agnostic (not TypeScript)
Current step highlighted in pseudocode during playback
Pseudocode clearly labels the critical section

title: Add pseudocode display to Producer-Consumer implementation
description:
I6=0/3: No pseudocode. The bounded buffer pattern with wait/signal is the canonical teaching example for condition variables. Add:
```
produce(item):
  lock(mutex)
  while buffer.full():
    wait(not_full, mutex)   // release lock, sleep until signaled
  buffer.add(item)
  signal(not_empty)         // wake a consumer
  unlock(mutex)

consume():
  lock(mutex)
  while buffer.empty():
    wait(not_empty, mutex)  // release lock, sleep until signaled
  item = buffer.remove()
  signal(not_full)          // wake a producer
  unlock(mutex)
  return item
```

NOTE: This pseudocode shows the condition variable pattern that our simulation REPRESENTS but doesn't explicitly model. The pseudocode teaches the synchronization mechanism; the visualization shows the behavior.
acceptanceCriteria: Pseudocode shown with wait/signal on condition variables
The while-loop guard (not if-check) is highlighted as critical
Pseudocode labels not_full and not_empty conditions explicitly

title: Add pseudocode display to Dining Philosophers, Deadlock Demo, Readers-Writers
description:
I6=0/3 for all three. These are classic textbook problems with well-known pseudocode in OSTEP and Tanenbaum. Add:

Dining Philosophers (naive):
```
philosopher(id):
  while true:
    think()
    pick_up(left_fork)
    pick_up(right_fork)  // blocks → potential deadlock
    eat()
    put_down(left_fork)
    put_down(right_fork)
```

Deadlock Demo:
```
thread_A():
  lock(R1)
  lock(R2)  // blocks if B holds R2
  // critical section
  unlock(R2); unlock(R1)

thread_B():
  lock(R2)
  lock(R1)  // blocks if A holds R1 → DEADLOCK
```

Readers-Writers:
```
reader():
  lock(mutex)
  readers++
  if readers == 1: lock(write_lock)
  unlock(mutex)
  // read shared data
  lock(mutex)
  readers--
  if readers == 0: unlock(write_lock)
  unlock(mutex)
```
acceptanceCriteria: All 3 concepts show pseudocode matching the OSTEP/Tanenbaum formulation
Dining Philosophers shows both naive and ordered pseudocode
Pseudocode highlights the deadlock-prone line with a comment
Readers-Writers pseudocode shows the readers-preference bias explicitly

title: Add missing legend to Dining Philosophers visualization
description:
V4=0/3: DiningPhilosophersViz has 4 philosopher state colors (thinking=#6b7280, hungry=#f59e0b, eating=#22c55e, waiting=#ef4444) and 2 fork states (free=#4b5563, held=#f59e0b) but NO legend explaining these. Students must guess what the colors mean. The PHILOSOPHER_STATES map at line 151-156 defines them but they're not shown in the UI.

Add a compact legend below or beside each SVG table showing:
• Gray circle = Thinking
• Amber circle = Hungry
• Green circle = Eating
• Red circle = Waiting
• Gray rectangle = Fork (free)
• Amber rectangle = Fork (held)
acceptanceCriteria: Legend visible for both naive and ordered philosopher tables
All 4 philosopher states and 2 fork states included
Legend uses the same colors as the SVG elements
Legend does not overlap or clip the SVG visualization

title: Add missing legend to Sleeping Barber and Readers-Writers visualizations
description:
V4=0/3 for both. SleepingBarberViz uses 6 action colors (BARBER_ACTION_COLORS at line 1491-1498) and Readers-Writers uses 5 action colors (RW_ACTION_COLORS at line 1324-1329) in their event logs, but neither has a legend explaining what each color means. Students see colored text lines but must infer the meaning.

Add a compact color legend (inline or collapsible) showing:
Sleeping Barber: enter=gray, wake-barber=green, sit-wait=blue, get-haircut=purple, done=green, leave-full=red
Readers-Writers: read-start=blue, read-end=light-blue, write-start=red, write-end=light-red, wait=amber
acceptanceCriteria: Color legend visible in both Sleeping Barber and Readers-Writers visualizations
Legend matches the exact colors used in the event log text
Legend is compact (one line or collapsible) and doesn't clutter the event log

title: Add Goroutine basic-channel step 4 clarification — unbuffered channels don't buffer values
description:
V1 semantic issue: In goroutines.ts basicChannel() tick 4, the channel buffer shows [42] after the rendezvous. But in real Go, unbuffered channels transfer values DIRECTLY from sender to receiver — the value never sits in a buffer. Our visualization shows it briefly in the buffer for visual effect, which could mislead students into thinking unbuffered channels have a temporary buffer.

FIX: Either (1) remove the [42] from the buffer and instead show a direct arrow transfer animation, or (2) add a note in the step description: 'Value 42 passes directly from sender to main (shown in buffer for illustration — real unbuffered channels have no buffer).'
acceptanceCriteria: Either buffer shows empty during unbuffered rendezvous, or description explicitly notes the visualization simplification
Student cannot confuse unbuffered channel behavior with buffered channel behavior

title: Enrich Race Condition step descriptions with 'WHY' — explain stale-read consequence at each step
description:
I5 teaching quality: Current steps say 'thread-0 reads counter = 0' (WHAT). The description includes '(based on stale read 0)' for writes which IS good, but read steps don't explain WHY a stale read is dangerous.

REWRITE examples:
- Old: 'thread-0 reads counter = 0'
- New: 'thread-0 reads counter = 0 into its local register. This value is now STALE — if another thread modifies counter before thread-0 writes back, thread-0 won't know.'

- Old: 'thread-1 reads counter = 0'
- New: 'thread-1 ALSO reads counter = 0. Both threads now hold the same stale value. When they both write back counter+1, one write will be lost.'

The WRITE steps already have good WHY ('based on stale read'). Focus on enriching READ steps.
acceptanceCriteria: All READ events in unsafeIncrement include a WHY clause about stale values
First read of each round explicitly says 'this value may become stale'
Second+ reads of each round explicitly say 'ALSO reads same stale value as thread-0'
Safe version READ events say 'protected by mutex — value guaranteed fresh'

title: Enrich Producer-Consumer step descriptions with WHY — explain wait/signal reasons
description:
I5 teaching: Current steps say 'producer-1 produces item 3 (buffer: 3/4)' — WHAT, not WHY. Rewrite to explain the synchronization decision at each step.

- Old: 'producer-1 waits (buffer full, 4/4)'
- New: 'producer-1 tries to add an item but buffer is FULL (4/4). It must WAIT — adding more items would corrupt the buffer. This is back-pressure: the producer is slowed to match consumer speed.'

- Old: 'consumer-1 signals: buffer not full'
- New: 'consumer-1 signals NOT_FULL condition. This wakes any blocked producer — the buffer now has space for new items.'
acceptanceCriteria: All produce events explain why the producer CAN produce (buffer has space)
All wait-full events explain the consequence of overflow
All signal events explain which condition is being signaled and who it wakes
Consumer wait-empty events explain why blocking prevents busy-waiting

title: Enrich Dining Philosophers step descriptions with WHY — connect to Coffman conditions
description:
Steps say 'P0 picks up left fork (fork 0)' — WHAT. Should explain WHY this leads to deadlock, connecting each action to the Coffman conditions being satisfied.

- Old: 'P0 picks up left fork (fork 0)'
- New: 'P0 picks up left fork (fork 0) and holds it while trying for the right. This is HOLD-AND-WAIT — one of the four conditions needed for deadlock.'

- Old: 'P2 waiting for right fork (fork 3)'
- New: 'P2 is waiting for fork 3, which is held by P3. But P3 is waiting for fork 4, held by P4... This CIRCULAR WAIT is forming — the last Coffman condition.'

- Old: 'DEADLOCK: All philosophers hold left fork and wait for right'
- New: 'DEADLOCK! All four Coffman conditions are now met: (1) Mutual exclusion — forks can't be shared. (2) Hold and wait — each philosopher holds one fork while waiting. (3) No preemption — forks can't be forcibly taken. (4) Circular wait — P0→P1→P2→P3→P4→P0.'
acceptanceCriteria: Each fork-pick-up step names the Coffman condition being satisfied
The circular wait formation is narrated step-by-step as it forms
The final DEADLOCK step explicitly lists all 4 conditions met
Ordered variant steps explain WHY ordering prevents circular wait

title: Add Readers-Writers writer-preference variant implementation
description:
I8=1/3: Only reader-preference policy implemented. Missing writer-preference (writers starve readers) and fair/FIFO policy. The readers-writers problem's entire teaching value is in comparing the three policies.

Add to readers-writers.ts:
1. simulateWriterPreference() — writers admitted as soon as resource is free, readers blocked while any writer is waiting
2. simulateFairPolicy() — FIFO ordering, neither side starved

Add sub-demo tabs in the visualization (like Event Loop's 3 demos).
acceptanceCriteria: Writer-preference variant shows reader starvation under heavy write load
Fair/FIFO variant shows no starvation for either side
Sub-demo tabs allow switching between reader-pref, writer-pref, and fair
Properties panel compares all 3 policies

title: Add Dining Philosophers Chandy/Misra variant implementation
description:
I8=1/3: Only naive + resource ordering. Missing Chandy/Misra (clean/dirty token passing) — the most elegant distributed deadlock prevention algorithm. Teaching value: shows that deadlock can be prevented without global coordination.

Add to dining-philosophers.ts:
simulateChangdyMisra(philosopherCount, ticks) — forks start dirty, philosopher must request clean fork from neighbor, neighbor gives up dirty fork, cleans it, and sends it.
acceptanceCriteria: Chandy/Misra variant added as sub-demo tab
Fork state shows clean/dirty in addition to owner
Properties panel explains why distributed prevention matters
Step descriptions explain the clean/dirty token protocol

title: Add multi-representation: Python display code for top 5 concurrency patterns
description:
I7=0/3 for 9 of 12 implementations. Students want to see code in THEIR language. Add Python display code panels for the 5 most universal patterns:
1. Race Condition: threading.Thread + shared counter
2. Producer-Consumer: threading.Condition + queue
3. Dining Philosophers: threading.Lock + resource ordering
4. Deadlock: threading.Lock + circular acquisition
5. Readers-Writers: threading.RLock + threading.Event

Display as a collapsible 'Python Code' panel alongside the visualization. Code should match the simulation logic, not be production-optimized.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 concepts show Python code in a collapsible panel
Python code uses threading module (standard library, no external deps)
Code matches the simulation's behavior (same race condition, same deadlock)
Code has inline comments explaining the concurrency-relevant lines

title: Add multi-representation: Java and Go display code for top 5 patterns
description:
After Python (CON-153), add Java (synchronized, ReentrantLock, Semaphore, Condition) and Go (goroutine, chan, sync.Mutex, sync.WaitGroup) display code for the same 5 patterns. Java and Go are the two most common languages in concurrency interviews.

Display as additional tabs in the code panel: Python | Java | Go
acceptanceCriteria: Java code uses java.util.concurrent classes (ReentrantLock, Condition, Semaphore)
Go code uses goroutines, channels, sync package
All 3 languages show the same logical behavior for each pattern
Tab switching preserves scroll position and current step

title: Add real-world production code comparison for Lock Comparison concept
description:
I9=0/3 across entire module. Lock Comparison is the best candidate for production code comparison because it directly maps to real implementations:
- SpinLock → Linux kernel spinlock_t (CONFIG_PREEMPT_NONE)
- Mutex → pthread_mutex / Java ReentrantLock / Go sync.Mutex
- TTAS → parking_lot::Mutex (Rust) / adaptive mutex (glibc)

Add a 'Production Code' section showing how each lock type is implemented in real systems, with key differences from our teaching version (e.g., 'our simulation uses 4 cycles/spin; real spinlocks use PAUSE instruction to reduce bus contention').
acceptanceCriteria: Production implementation referenced for each of 3 lock types
Key differences between teaching version and production version noted
At least 1 specific production system named per lock type
Student understands the gap between 'textbook' and 'production'

title: Add educational inline comments to Race Condition and Producer-Consumer engine code
description:
I10 varies. Race Condition has excellent file-level docs (I10=3) but Producer-Consumer could be better. More importantly, since these engines ARE the learning material (students may read the source), add inline 'WHY' comments at key decision points:

race-condition.ts: Add comment at line 59 explaining WHY we interleave all-reads-then-all-writes: 'We deliberately schedule ALL reads before ANY writes to guarantee the race is visible. In real systems, the interleaving is non-deterministic — this synthetic schedule shows the worst case.'

producer-consumer.ts: Add comment at buffer.length >= bufferSize explaining: 'This is the full-buffer guard. In a real implementation, this would be a condition variable wait: while(buffer.full()) cv.wait(mutex). Our simulation models the behavior without the explicit sync primitive.'
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 'WHY' comments added to race-condition.ts explaining design choices
At least 3 'WHY' comments added to producer-consumer.ts explaining sync patterns
Comments explain what the REAL implementation would look like vs our simulation
Comments read as a narrative — reading just comments teaches the concept
